perturbation.original_text: HealthGate shall collect the Article Fees established by Publishers plus a service fee to be determined by HealthGate but in any event the service fee may not exceed 30% of the Article Fee for the particular article, or $US 4, whichever is the higher. Within 60 days of the end of each calendar month, HealthGate shall forward to Publishers the net Article Fees actually collected (exclusive of HealthGate's service fee).
perturbation.changed_text: HealthGate shall collect the Article Fees established by Publishers plus a service fee to be determined by HealthGate but in any event the service fee may not exceed 30% of the Article Fee for the particular article, or $US 4, whichever is the higher. HealthGate shall forward to Publishers the net Article Fees actually collected (exclusive of HealthGate's service fee) at the company's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment timeline to 'at the company's discretion' is a contradiction because it violates the requirement of payment timelines under Massachusetts law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts M.G.L. c. 149 § 148, which mandates definite payment schedules. Changing the payment timeline to 'at the company's discretion' introduces uncertainty and violates this law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 149, Section 148 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 149 LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Section 148 Payment of wages; commissions; exemption by contract; persons deemed employers; provision for cashing check or draft; violation of statute Section 148 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 148: Payment of wages; commissions; exemption by contract; persons deemed employers; provision for cashing check or draft; violation of statute Section 148. Every person having employees in his service shall pay weekly or bi-weekly each such employee the wages earned by him to within six days of the termination of the pay period during which the wages were earned if employed for five or six days in a calendar week, or to within seven days of the termination of the pay period during which the wages were earned if such employee is employed seven days in a calendar week, or in the case of an employee who has worked for a period of less than five days, hereinafter called a casual employee, shall, within seven days after the termination of such period, pay the wages earned by such casual employee during such period, but any employee leaving his employment shall be paid in full on the following regular pay day, and, in the absence of a regular pay day, on the following Saturday; and any employee discharged from such employment shall be paid in full on the day of his discharge, or in Boston as soon as the laws requiring pay rolls, bills and accounts to be certified shall have been complied with; and the commonwealth, its departments, officers, boards and commissions shall so pay every mechanic, workman and laborer employed by it or them, and every person employed in any other capacity by it or them in any penal or charitable institution, and every county and city shall so pay every employee engaged in its business the wag

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by making the payment timeline discretionary, whereas the original text specified a 60-day timeline. This violates the principle of definite payment schedules as suggested by the law explanation. The scraped law snippets from M.G.L. c. 149 § 148 state that employers 'shall pay weekly or bi-weekly each such employee the wages earned by him'. Although the snippets explicitly refer to employee wages, the underlying principle of mandated payment schedules contradicts the discretionary payment term introduced in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: Invoices are payable within 60 days of receipt, with the exception of payments due under Clause 19.2, which shall be payable on the due date or on acceptance of the work, which ever is the later.
perturbation.changed_text: Invoices are payable within 120 days of receipt, with the exception of payments due under Clause 19.2, which shall be payable on the due date or on acceptance of the work, which ever is the later.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the payment timeline for invoices to 120 days introduces a contradiction because it violates the prompt payment standards under Massachusetts law for construction contracts (even though this agreement is not for construction, the principle of prompt payment is established).
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the principle of prompt payment in M.G.L. c. 149, § 29E. While this law explicitly addresses construction contracts, the underlying principle that payment should be made promptly is generally applicable. Extending the payment period to 120 days is an unreasonable delay.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 149, Section 29E Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 149 LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Section 29E Construction contracts; reasonable time periods for periodic progress payments and increases in contract price; payment conditioned upon receipt of payment from third party; requirement to continue performance of construction without payment Section 29E Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 29E: Construction contracts; reasonable time periods for periodic progress payments and increases in contract price; payment conditioned upon receipt of payment from third party; requirement to continue performance of construction without payment Section 29E. (a) As used in this section the following words shall have the following meanings, unless the context clearly requires otherwise: ''Contract for construction'', a contract for which a lien may be established under sections 2 or 4 of chapter 254 on a project for which the person whose contract with the project owner has an original contract price of $3,000,000 or more; provided, however, this shall not include projects containing or designed to contain at least 1 but not more than 4 dwelling units. ''Insolvent'', insolvent as defined under federal bankruptcy law or being a debtor in a proceeding commenced under federal bankruptcy law or under the corresponding law of another country, or being a debtor in a receivership proceeding or having made an assignment for the benefit of creditors. (b) A communication required in this section to be in writing may be submitted in electronic form and by electronic means. perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the payment timeline for invoices from 60 to 120 days, which contradicts the principle of prompt payment, as interpreted in the law explanation. Although the law snippets (M.G.L. c. 149, § 29E) explicitly pertain to construction contracts, they establish the requirement for "reasonable time periods" for payments. The perturbation explanation argues that extending the payment period to 120 days is an unreasonable delay, conflicting with the general intent of prompt payment even if the contract in question is not a construction contract. Therefore, even though the direct application of the law is limited, the principle it embodies is undermined.

perturbation.original_text: 33.3. Upon receipt of written notice from the Publishers that HealthGate is in default under this Agreement by failing to comply with the requirements of this Clause 33, or that HealthGate is in default under any provision regarding rights upon termination of this Agreement, HealthGate shall have ten (10) business days in which to cure such default.
perturbation.changed_text: 33.3. Upon receipt of written notice from the Publishers that HealthGate is in default under this Agreement by failing to comply with the requirements of this Clause 33, or that HealthGate is in default under any provision regarding rights upon termination of this Agreement, HealthGate shall have two (2) business days in which to cure such default.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to two business days is a contradiction because it provides an unreasonably short timeframe to remedy a default, potentially violating principles of fairness and good faith under Massachusetts contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the principles of fairness and good faith implied in Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A. While 93A primarily addresses unfair or deceptive business practices, reducing the cure period to two business days could be seen as an unfair practice, particularly if the default is complex and requires more time to resolve.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the cure period from ten to two business days. The Law Explanation indicates this timeframe could be deemed unfair under Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A. The Perturbation Explanation says that a reduced cure period potentially violates principles of fairness and good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows that Chapter 93A covers 'Regulation of Business Practices for Consumers Protection' and addresses 'Unfair practices'. The reduction to two days could be considered an unfair practice, especially if the default is complex, thus creating a contradiction.
